mcqs.question: What is the primary goal of page replacement algorithms?

options.text: Minimize page fault rate
Maximize CPU utilization
Reduce disk I/O operations
Increase memory capacity

mcqs.explanation: The main objective of page replacement algorithms is to minimize the page fault rate by selecting the best page to replace when no free frames are available.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm suffers from Belady's Anomaly?

options.text: Optimal (OPT)
Least Recently Used (LRU)
First-In-First-Out (FIFO)
Clock algorithm

mcqs.explanation: FIFO is the only algorithm among these that exhibits Belady's Anomaly, where increasing the number of frames can sometimes increase the page fault rate.

mcqs.question: In the Clock page replacement algorithm, what happens when a page's reference bit is 1?

options.text: The page is immediately replaced
The reference bit is set to 0 and the algorithm moves to the next page
The page is marked as most recently used
The page is written to disk if modified

mcqs.explanation: In the Clock algorithm, if a page's reference bit is 1, it is given a 'second chance' - the reference bit is set to 0 and the algorithm moves to the next page in the circular list.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements about the Optimal (OPT) page replacement algorithm is TRUE?

options.text: It is commonly used in modern operating systems
It requires knowledge of the future reference string
It has higher overhead than LRU implementation
It performs worse than FIFO in most cases

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm requires knowledge of the future page reference string, which makes it impossible to implement in practice. It is used only as a theoretical benchmark.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the dirty bit in page replacement?

options.text: To indicate which page was most recently used
To track how many times a page has been referenced
To identify pages that need to be written to disk when replaced
To mark pages that should never be replaced

mcqs.explanation: The dirty bit (or modify bit) indicates whether a page has been modified since it was loaded into memory. If set, the page must be written back to disk when replaced.
